schools.summary: God tests Abraham: 'and it came to pass after these things, that God did tempt Abraham, and said unto him, Abraham: and he said, Behold, here I am' — 'God did tempt (tried, proved, tested) Abraham' (God not tempting him to evil, for 'God cannot be tempted with evil, neither tempteth he any man,' James 1:13, but trying and proving his faith and obedience, the 'tempting' here a testing, a trial of the patriarch's faith, as God proved Job, the great trial of Abraham, to bring forth and display his faith, the testing that distinguishes God's trying of the godly from Satan's tempting to sin); the trial a real testing (God commanding what would prove Abraham's faith to the uttermost, the offering of Isaac, v.2, the former trials, the call, 12:1, the long wait for the seed, not superseding this, the greatest, the proving of the patriarch's faith, the trial that, though severe, was for Abraham's good and for a witness to all, Hebrews 11:17); 'Behold, here I am' (Abraham's answer showing his ready, willing attendance on God's call, the prompt obedience of the servant, present and attentive, the readiness of faith to hear and obey, before he knew the hard command, v.2) — Henry, Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'God did tempt Abraham' mean?

sides.position: A testing/trying of Abraham's faith and obedience, not a tempting to evil (God tempts no man to sin, James 1:13) - the proving of the godly, as God tried Job, to display Abraham's faith (Hebrews 11:17)

themes: And it came to pass after these things, that God did tempt Abraham... and he said, Behold, here I am
God's testing of Abraham (the crux of 'tempt')
a testing/trying of faith and obedience, not a tempting to evil (James 1:13)
the proving of the godly, to display Abraham's faith (Hebrews 11:17)

schools.summary: The command to offer Isaac: 'and he said, Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest, and get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of' — the words heaped up (thy son, thine only son, whom thou lovest, even Isaac) to heighten the severity of the trial (the command touching all that was dearest to Abraham — his son, his only son, by Sarah, the son of promise, the son he loved, the trial pressed to the uttermost, the command to offer the very child of promise, through whom all the blessings hung, 21:12); God commanding Abraham to 'offer him for a burnt offering' — to sacrifice Isaac (the command, on its face, seeming to contradict God's promise, the seed through Isaac, 21:12, and the law against murder, 9:6, yet a trial, and God never intending the actual death, he provided the ram, v.13, and Abraham believed God could raise Isaac, Hebrews 11:19, God, the Lord of life, may command what he wills, and the command to prove Abraham, not to be carried to the death, the moral difficulty resolved in that God stayed the hand and provided the substitute); the offering of the only beloved son a striking type of the sacrifice of Christ (the Father offering his only-begotten, beloved Son, John 3:16; Romans 8:32, 'he that spared not his own Son,' Isaac, the only son whom Abraham loved, offered, in figure, and received back as from the dead, Hebrews 11:19, 'in a figure,' foreshadowing the death and resurrection of Christ, the land of Moriah, where the temple would stand, 2 Chronicles 3:1, near Calvary, the place of the offering, the type of the great sacrifice) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Henry.

debates.question: How is the command to offer Isaac to be understood (the trial and the type)?

sides.position: A supreme trial of faith - God never intending the actual death (the ram provided, v.13; Abraham believing in the resurrection, Hebrews 11:19), the moral difficulty resolved by the staying of the hand
The offering of the only beloved son a type of the Father's offering of his only Son, Christ, on Moriah (John 3:16; Romans 8:32)

themes: And he said, Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest... and offer him there for a burnt offering
the command to offer Isaac (the crux of the trial and the type)
a supreme trial of faith, God never intending the death (the ram provided; Hebrews 11:19)
the only beloved son a type of the Father's offering of his only Son, Christ (John 3:16; Romans 8:32)

schools.summary: Abraham's prompt obedience: 'and Abraham rose up early in the morning, and saddled his ass, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son, and clave the wood for the burnt offering, and rose up, and went unto the place of which God had told him' — the patriarch obeying without delay, rising early to do the hard thing (the prompt, unquestioning obedience of faith, despite the anguish the command must have caused, the readiness to obey God at once, however grievous, the obedience that 'staggered not at the promise... through unbelief,' Romans 4:20); Abraham making all the preparations himself — saddling the ass, taking the young men and Isaac, cleaving the wood (the deliberate, methodical obedience, the patriarch attending to every detail of the dreadful task, the steadfastness of his resolve); 'went unto the place of which God had told him' (Abraham setting out for Moriah, v.2, obeying God's direction, the journey of obedience and faith, the patriarch going, not knowing fully how God would fulfill the promise, yet obeying, Hebrews 11:8, 17-19, the obedience of faith carried out) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and saddled his ass... and went unto the place of which God had told him
Abraham's prompt obedience to the hard command
the prompt, unquestioning obedience of faith, without delay (Romans 4:20)
the steadfast obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:17-19)

schools.summary: Abraham sees the place on the third day: 'then on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off' — after three days' journey, Abraham seeing the place (Moriah) at a distance (the three days marking the length of the trial, the patriarch's resolve held through three days' anguish, the place of the offering coming into view); the 'third day' marking the prolonged trial (Abraham's faith and obedience tested through three days of journeying with the son he was to offer, the steadfastness of his resolve, not failing through the long ordeal, some seeing in the three days a figure of the time between Isaac's being, as it were, given up to death and his being received back, Hebrews 11:19, answering to the three days of Christ); 'saw the place afar off' (Abraham recognizing the place God had appointed, Moriah, v.2, the place of the offering in view, the patriarch nearing the dreadful task, the steadfast approach to obey) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: Then on the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place afar off
Abraham seeing the place of sacrifice on the third day
the three days marking the prolonged trial of faith
the three days a figure, some say, of the death and resurrection (Hebrews 11:19)

schools.summary: Abraham's word of faith - 'we will come again': 'and Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship, and come again to you' — Abraham leaving the young men with the ass and going on alone with Isaac to the place (the patriarch keeping the dreadful task private, calling the offering 'worship,' the sacrifice an act of worship and obedience to God, the going up to worship); Abraham saying 'and come again to you' ('I and the lad will... come again,' a remarkable word of faith — Abraham, resolved to offer Isaac, yet expecting to come again with him, whether spoken in faith that God would raise Isaac from the dead, Hebrews 11:19, 'accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead; from whence also he received him in a figure,' or that God would somehow restore him, the faith that, even in obeying the command to slay, held to the promise, that the seed should be through Isaac, 21:12, the word betraying Abraham's confidence that Isaac would live); Abraham's 'come again to you' the language of faith (he believed God would fulfill the promise through Isaac, even if he must offer him, the faith that reconciled the command and the promise by trusting God's power to raise the dead, Hebrews 11:19, the patriarch's unshaken faith, that the son of promise would live) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham said unto his young men, Abide ye here with the ass; and I and the lad will go yonder and worship, and come again to you
Abraham's words of faith - 'we will come again'
the patriarch expecting to return with Isaac, believing God could raise the dead (Hebrews 11:19)
the faith that held command and promise together

schools.summary: Isaac bears the wood: 'and Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife; and they went both of them together' — Abraham laying the wood for the sacrifice on Isaac to carry (Isaac, a youth, strong enough to bear the wood, carrying the wood of his own intended offering, a striking figure — Isaac bearing the wood as Christ bore his cross, John 19:17, the type of the Son carrying the wood of his own sacrifice); Abraham carrying the fire and the knife (the instruments of the sacrifice) in his own hand (the dreadful means of the offering borne by the father, the patriarch ready to perform the sacrifice); 'they went both of them together' (father and son going on together to the place, the touching scene — the father bearing the fire and knife, the son the wood, both going to the offering, the unsuspecting Isaac and the resolved Abraham, the type of the Father and the Son together in the work of redemption, the going up to the sacrifice) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering, and laid it upon Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife; and they went both of them together
Isaac bearing the wood as they went together
Isaac bearing the wood of his offering, as Christ bore his cross (John 19:17)
the father and son going up to the sacrifice together

schools.summary: Isaac's question about the lamb: 'and Isaac spake unto Abraham his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I, my son. And he said, Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?' — the touching exchange between father and son (Isaac's address, 'My father,' and Abraham's tender answer, 'Here am I, my son,' the affection between them, heightening the pathos of the scene, the father about to offer the son who called him 'father'); Isaac asking, 'Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?' (the lad, seeing the fire and wood but no victim, asking where the lamb was, the innocent, unsuspecting question, piercing the father's heart, Isaac not yet knowing he himself was the intended offering, the question that drew out Abraham's word of faith, v.8); Isaac's question — innocent and trusting — must have cut Abraham deeply (the father bearing the dreadful knowledge while the son asked for the lamb, the pathos of the scene, the question answered by Abraham's faith that God would provide, v.8) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Isaac spake unto Abraham his father... Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?
Isaac's question about the lamb
the tender exchange between father and son, heightening the pathos
the innocent question piercing the father's heart, drawing out his word of faith

schools.summary: God will provide himself a lamb: 'and Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering: so they went both of them together' — 'God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering' Abraham's answer to Isaac, a word of faith and (as it proved) of prophecy: God himself would provide the offering (Abraham trusting that God would furnish the victim, and God did, the ram, v.13, the unshaken faith of the patriarch, leaving the provision to God, the word reconciling his obedience, offering Isaac, with his trust, that God would provide); Abraham's word a prophecy — fulfilled immediately in the ram (v.13), and ultimately in Christ, 'the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world' (John 1:29), whom God would provide as the true offering, the word 'God will provide himself a lamb' looking beyond the ram to the great Sacrifice, the Lamb whom God himself would furnish (the Father providing the Son, John 3:16; 1 Peter 1:19-20), the type of Christ, the Lamb provided by God, 'Jehovah-jireh,' v.14, the LORD will provide); 'so they went both of them together' (father and son going on together to the place, Abraham's faith resting in God's provision, the resolved obedience continuing, the going up to the offering, in the faith that God would provide) — Clarke, Henry, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'God will provide himself a lamb' signify?

sides.position: Abraham's word of faith - trusting God to provide the victim, fulfilled in the ram (v.13)
A prophecy/type of Christ, the Lamb of God whom God himself would provide as the true offering (John 1:29; John 3:16; 1 Peter 1:19-20)

themes: And Abraham said, My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering: so they went both of them together
Abraham's word, 'God will provide himself a lamb' (the crux of the faith and the type)
Abraham's word of faith, fulfilled in the ram (v.13)
a prophecy/type of Christ, the Lamb of God whom God would provide (John 1:29; John 3:16)

schools.summary: Abraham builds the altar and binds Isaac: 'and they came to the place which God had told him of; and Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood' — at Moriah, Abraham building the altar and arranging the wood for the offering (the deliberate, resolved obedience, carrying out every step of the dreadful task, the patriarch preparing the sacrifice of his son); Abraham 'bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood' (the binding of Isaac, the 'Akedah' — the son bound and laid on the altar, ready for the knife, Isaac's submission, he, a strong youth, submitting to be bound by his aged father, a willing victim — a striking type of Christ, who willingly gave himself, John 10:18; Philippians 2:8, the resolved obedience of the father and the submission of the son, the supreme moment of the trial); the binding and laying of Isaac on the altar — the son a willing, submissive victim — foreshadowing Christ, who was bound and offered, the willing Lamb (Isaiah 53:7; Acts 8:32, the obedience of Isaac, submitting to his father, a figure of the obedience of Christ, to the Father, the type of the great Sacrifice) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And they came to the place... and Abraham built an altar there, and laid the wood in order, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar upon the wood
Abraham building the altar and binding Isaac (the Akedah)
Isaac the willing, submissive victim, a type of Christ (John 10:18; Philippians 2:8)
the resolved obedience of the father and the submission of the son

schools.summary: Abraham reaches for the knife: 'and Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son' — Abraham, at the last moment, reaching out his hand and taking the knife, ready to slay Isaac (the supreme test of obedience, carried to the very point of the act, the patriarch's faith and obedience proved to the uttermost — he would have done it, but for God's intervention, v.11, the resolved hand raised over the bound son); Abraham's stretching forth his hand to take the knife showing the completeness of his obedience (he held nothing back, but was about to slay his son, the faith that would obey to the death, the trial at its height); 'to slay his son' (Abraham on the very point of slaying Isaac, the obedience proved to the last, God reckoning the act as done in Abraham's resolved will, v.12, 'thou hast not withheld thy son'; Hebrews 11:17, 'offered up Isaac,' the supreme moment, the hand raised, before God stayed it, v.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Abraham stretched forth his hand, and took the knife to slay his son
Abraham reaching for the knife to slay Isaac
the obedience proved to the uttermost, holding nothing back
the act reckoned as done in Abraham's resolved will (Hebrews 11:17)

schools.summary: The angel of the LORD stays Abraham's hand: 'and the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham: and he said, Here am I' — at the very moment of the act (v.10), the Angel of the LORD calling from heaven to stop Abraham (the timely intervention, the Angel, who speaks as God, v.12, 16, 'thou hast not withheld thy son from me'; 'by myself have I sworn,' by the dominant reading a divine Person, the Angel of the LORD, the pre-incarnate Son, as 16:7-13, God himself staying the hand); the doubled name — 'Abraham, Abraham' (the urgent, emphatic call, as to Moses, Exodus 3:4; Samuel, 1 Samuel 3:10, the urgency of the moment, the hand about to fall, the affectionate, arresting call to stay the patriarch); Abraham's answer, 'Here am I' (as v.1, showing his ready attention even at the supreme moment, the obedient servant, attentive to God's voice, the patriarch arrested in the act, ready to hear God's word, v.12) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, Abraham, Abraham: and he said, Here am I
the angel of the LORD staying Abraham's hand
the Angel a divine Person (16:7-13; v.12, 16), God himself staying the hand
the urgent, doubled call at the supreme moment

schools.summary: God stays the hand and approves Abraham's fear: 'and he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him: for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me' — God forbidding Abraham to harm Isaac ('Lay not thine hand upon the lad,' the actual sacrifice not required, it was the trial, not the death, that God sought, the staying of the hand, the son spared, God, having proved Abraham, would not have the death); 'now I know that thou fearest God' (God speaking after the manner of men: not that God learned anything he did not know, he knows all hearts, but that Abraham's fear of God was now proved, displayed, made evident by the act, the 'knowing' the manifestation of what God knew, shown to Abraham and to all, the trial bringing forth and approving Abraham's faith and fear of God, the fear of God meaning his reverent, obedient devotion); 'thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me' (Abraham's not withholding Isaac, his readiness to offer even his only son, the proof of his fear and faith, the act reckoned as done, Hebrews 11:17, 'offered up Isaac,' the supreme proof of devotion — withholding nothing, not even the dearest, from God, the type, again, of the Father who 'spared not his own Son,' Romans 8:32) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad... for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me
God staying the hand and approving Abraham's fear
'now I know' an anthropomorphism - Abraham's fear proved and displayed, not learned by God
the not-withholding of the only son, the type of the Father who spared not his Son (Romans 8:32)

schools.summary: The ram offered in Isaac's stead: 'and Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns: and Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son' — God providing a ram, caught by its horns in a thicket, for the offering (the fulfillment of Abraham's word, v.8, 'God will provide himself a lamb,' the substitute provided by God at the very moment, the ram caught and ready); Abraham offering the ram 'for a burnt offering in the stead of his son' (the ram offered in Isaac's place, the substitution — the ram dying instead of Isaac, the burnt offering completed with the substitute, the son spared, the ram offered); the ram offered 'in the stead of his son' a striking figure of substitution (the victim dying in the place of the condemned, the type of Christ, who died in the stead of sinners, the substitutionary sacrifice, Isaiah 53:5-6; 1 Peter 3:18, 'the just for the unjust,' Isaac, the bound son, spared, and the ram, the provided victim, offered in his place, the gospel of substitution foreshadowed, the Lamb of God dying in the sinner's stead) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham lifted up his eyes... and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns: and Abraham... offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son
the ram provided and offered in Isaac's stead
God providing the substitute (the word of v.8 fulfilled)
the substitution a type of Christ who died in the sinner's stead (Isaiah 53:5-6; 1 Peter 3:18)

schools.summary: The naming of Jehovah-jireh: 'and Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh: as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen' — Abraham naming the place Jehovah-jireh (the LORD will provide, or the LORD will see/be seen), commemorating God's provision of the ram (v.13) in answer to his word of faith (v.8, 'God will provide,' the name a memorial of the deliverance and the provision, 'jireh,' from raah, to see/provide, 'the LORD will see, provide'); 'as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen' the phrase becoming a proverb, variously rendered: (a) 'In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen (or provided)' — in the place of difficulty, God will be seen to provide (as he had provided the ram), the proverb of trust, that God provides in the extremity; (b) 'In the mount the LORD will be seen (will appear)' — pointing to God's appearing/manifestation on the mount (and, by some, to the future — Moriah, where the temple would stand, 2 Chronicles 3:1, and near Calvary, where the great provision, Christ, would be seen); (c) the margin 'The LORD will see,' 'The LORD will provide,' God's provision and foresight, the dominant sense — God provides (and is seen to provide) in the mount, in the place of need; Jehovah-jireh, 'the LORD will provide,' the memorial of God's provision (the ram), and (as the proverb suggests) a standing testimony that God provides for his people in their extremity, some seeing a prophecy — that in the mount of the LORD (Moriah/Zion, Calvary) the great provision (Christ, the Lamb) would be seen — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'Jehovah-jireh' and 'In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen' mean?

sides.position: 'The LORD will provide' - God will be seen to provide in the place of need (as he provided the ram); the proverb of trust that God provides in the extremity
'The LORD will be seen/appear in the mount' - pointing, by some, to God's manifestation and the future provision (Christ, the Lamb) on Moriah/Calvary

themes: And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovahjireh: as it is said to this day, In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen
the naming of Jehovah-jireh (the crux of the difficult phrase)
'the LORD will provide' (the ram, and in the place of need) vs 'the LORD will be seen/appear in the mount'
pointing, by some, to the future provision, Christ, on Moriah/Calvary

schools.summary: The angel calls a second time: 'and the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time' — the Angel of the LORD (who had stayed his hand, v.11) calling again from heaven, now to pronounce the great confirming oath of the covenant (vv.16-18, the second call, after the offering of the ram, v.13, the Angel, a divine Person, swearing by himself, v.16, renewing the promise with an oath); this the 'second time' the Angel called (the first to stay the hand, v.11, the second to bless and confirm, the renewed communication, the reward of Abraham's obedience now to be declared, vv.16-18, the confirming of the covenant with an oath); the second call introducing the solemn oath by which God confirmed his covenant promises to Abraham (vv.16-18, in reward for his obedience, the Angel of the LORD, the divine Person, swearing by himself, v.16, the great confirming oath of the Abrahamic covenant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time
the angel of the LORD calling Abraham a second time
the renewed call, now to pronounce the great confirming oath of the covenant
the reward of Abraham's obedience to be declared (vv.16-18)

schools.summary: God confirms the covenant with an oath: 'and said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son' — God confirming his covenant promises with an oath, swearing by himself (there being none greater to swear by, Hebrews 6:13, 'because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself,' the solemn, unchangeable oath of God, the two immutable things, the promise and the oath, by which God confirmed his word, Hebrews 6:17-18, the great confirming oath of the Abrahamic covenant); the Angel of the LORD speaking as the LORD ('saith the LORD,' swearing by himself, a token, with vv.11-12, that the Angel is a divine Person, the pre-incarnate Son, God himself confirming his promise with an oath); 'because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son' (the oath given in reward for Abraham's obedience, his offering of Isaac, not that the promise rested on Abraham's works, it was of grace, 15:6, but that his obedience, the fruit of faith, was crowned and rewarded, James 2:21-23, 'was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac... and the scripture was fulfilled... Abraham believed God,' the obedience of faith rewarded, the not-withholding of the only son the occasion of the confirming oath) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son
God's confirming the covenant with an oath
God swearing by himself, there being none greater (Hebrews 6:13, 17-18)
the oath rewarding Abraham's obedience of faith (James 2:21-23)

schools.summary: The promise of blessing and a numberless seed: 'that in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies' — the doubled Hebrew ('in blessing... bless,' 'in multiplying... multiply') marking the certainty and abundance of the promise (God would surely and abundantly bless Abraham and multiply his seed, the confirming of the blessing and the seed-promise with the oath); the double figure — 'as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand... upon the sea shore' (the seed of Abraham numberless, as the stars, 15:5, and the sand, the vast multitude of his descendants, natural, Israel, and spiritual, all the faithful, Romans 4:16-18; Galatians 3:7, 29, the immense fulfillment of the promise); 'thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies' (Abraham's seed would possess, conquer, hold, the gate, the strength, the cities, of their enemies, the victory and dominion of the seed, Israel over the Canaanites, and, supremely, Christ and his people over their spiritual enemies — sin, death, hell, the gates of which shall not prevail, Matthew 16:18, the triumph of the seed) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies
the promise of blessing and a numberless seed
the seed numberless as the stars and the sand (15:5; Romans 4:16-18)
the victory of the seed, possessing the gate of his enemies (Matthew 16:18)

schools.summary: The Gospel promise - all nations blessed in Abraham's seed: 'and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice' — the great gospel promise (12:3; 18:18) confirmed with the oath: all nations blessed in Abraham's seed (Paul declaring the 'seed' to be Christ — 'to thy seed, which is Christ,' Galatians 3:16, in Christ, of Abraham's line, all nations, Jew and Gentile, blessed with justification and salvation, the Gospel promise, the heart of the covenant, sworn to Abraham); 'thy seed' supremely Christ (Galatians 3:16, the promised Seed, the Messiah, in whom the blessing comes to all nations, the Gospel preached to Abraham, Galatians 3:8, the blessing of Abraham coming on the Gentiles through Christ, Galatians 3:14, the universal blessing in the one Seed); 'because thou hast obeyed my voice' (the oath and the renewed promise given in connection with Abraham's obedience, the offering of Isaac, not that the promise was earned, it was of grace, but that Abraham's obedience of faith was crowned, and the covenant confirmed to the obedient patriarch, James 2:21-23, the reward of the faith that obeys) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice
the Gospel promise - all nations blessed in Abraham's seed
'thy seed' supremely Christ (Galatians 3:16), in whom all nations are blessed
the reward of Abraham's obedience of faith (James 2:21-23)

schools.summary: Abraham returns to Beersheba: 'so Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba' — the trial over (the son spared, the covenant confirmed), Abraham returning to the young men (left at the foot of the mount, v.5) with Isaac, and going back to Beersheba (his dwelling, 21:33, the return in peace, the great trial accomplished, the patriarch dwelling again at Beersheba); Abraham returning with Isaac (the 'come again to you,' v.5, fulfilled, the son restored, as from the dead, Hebrews 11:19, the company reunited, the journey home in peace); 'Abraham dwelt at Beersheba' (the patriarch resuming his dwelling at Beersheba, 21:31-33, the peaceful settling after the trial, the great test past, Abraham at rest in his place, the close of the Akedah, the patriarch blessed and at peace) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: So Abraham returned unto his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba
Abraham returning to Beersheba after the trial
the son restored, 'come again' fulfilled (v.5; Hebrews 11:19)
the peaceful close of the Akedah, the patriarch home in peace

schools.summary: The news of Nahor's children: 'and it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham, saying, Behold, Milcah, she hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor' — Abraham receiving news of the family of his brother Nahor (who had remained in Mesopotamia, 11:27-29, Milcah, Nahor's wife, having borne him children, the report of the growing family of Nahor, the genealogy that prepares for Rebekah, v.23, Isaac's future wife); the news concerning Nahor, Abraham's brother (11:26-29), and his children (the family left behind in Haran, the connection maintained between Abraham and his kindred, the report bringing word of the home family); the genealogy of Nahor's children (vv.20-24) given chiefly to introduce Rebekah (v.23), the daughter of Bethuel (Nahor's son), who would become Isaac's wife (ch. 24, the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise, the news that sets the stage for the next great event) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass after these things, that it was told Abraham, saying, Behold, Milcah, she hath also born children unto thy brother Nahor
the news of the children of Nahor
the report of Abraham's brother's family (11:26-29)
the genealogy preparing for Rebekah (v.23), Isaac's future wife

schools.summary: The first of Nahor's sons: 'Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram' — the first three of Nahor's sons by Milcah: Huz (the firstborn, perhaps connected with the land of Uz, Job 1:1), Buz (perhaps connected with Buz, the kindred of Elihu, Job 32:2; Jeremiah 25:23), and Kemuel (the father of Aram, an Aramaean line, the sons of Nahor heading various peoples); the sons of Nahor becoming the heads of various Aramaean and Arabian tribes (Uz, Buz, Aram, the kindred of Abraham spreading into peoples, the families of the brother of the patriarch, the genealogy tracing Nahor's line); the sons of Nahor (vv.21-24) listed, leading to Bethuel and Rebekah (v.23, the genealogy of Abraham's kindred, the families related to the line of promise, from whom Isaac's wife, and later Jacob's wives, would come, the providence in the kindred of Abraham) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: Huz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram
the first of Nahor's sons
the sons of Nahor heading various peoples (Uz, Buz, Aram)
the genealogy leading toward Bethuel and Rebekah (v.23)

schools.summary: More of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel: 'and Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel' — more of the sons of Nahor by Milcah: Chesed (perhaps connected with the Chasdim/Chaldeans), Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel (named last, but the most significant for the history, being the father of Rebekah, v.23, the further sons of Nahor); the sons (Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph) heads of further families/tribes of Abraham's kindred (the genealogy listing Nahor's eight sons by Milcah, v.23, the families of the patriarch's brother); 'Bethuel' (named last, yet the most important for the sacred history, being the father of Rebekah, v.23, Isaac's future wife, and of Laban, the father of Leah and Rachel, the line through which the brides of the patriarchs would come, the providence preparing the family of the wife of the son of promise) — Gill, JFB, Ellicott.

themes: And Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel
more of Nahor's sons, including Bethuel
the heads of further families of Abraham's kindred
Bethuel the father of Rebekah (v.23), through whom Isaac's wife would come

schools.summary: Bethuel begets Rebekah: 'and Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother' — Bethuel (Nahor's son) begetting Rebekah (the eight sons Milcah bore to Nahor summed up, the notable note — Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel, who would become Isaac's wife, ch. 24, the purpose of the genealogy revealed in Rebekah); the chief point of the genealogy Rebekah (the granddaughter of Nahor, the future wife of Isaac, the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise, the family from which Isaac's wife, and later Jacob's wives, would come, the connection of the line of promise with the kindred of Abraham); 'these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother' (the eight sons of Nahor by his wife Milcah, vv.21-23, the family of Abraham's brother, the genealogy of the kindred, given to introduce Rebekah, the providence in the family of Nahor preparing for the marriage of Isaac, ch. 24) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Bethuel begat Rebekah: these eight Milcah did bear to Nahor, Abraham's brother
Bethuel begetting Rebekah, the future wife of Isaac
the providence preparing the bride for the son of promise (ch. 24)
the connection of the line of promise with the kindred of Abraham

schools.summary: The sons of Nahor's concubine: 'and his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah' — Nahor having, besides his sons by Milcah (vv.21-23), four sons by his concubine Reumah: Tebah, Gaham, Thahash, and Maachah (the further offspring of Nahor, the heads of further tribes/families of Abraham's kindred); Reumah Nahor's concubine (secondary wife, the sons by her, like those by Milcah, becoming heads of families/tribes, the full account of Nahor's offspring, the kindred of Abraham spreading into many peoples); 'Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah' (the sons of the concubine, heads of various Aramaean/Arabian tribes, the completing of the genealogy of Nahor, vv.20-24, the family of Abraham's brother fully recorded, the close of the chapter, having introduced Rebekah, v.23, for the coming marriage of Isaac, ch. 24) — Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And his concubine, whose name was Reumah, she bare also Tebah, and Gaham, and Thahash, and Maachah
the sons of Nahor's concubine
the further offspring of Nahor, heads of further tribes
the completing of the genealogy of Abraham's brother, which introduced Rebekah (v.23)
